16: vayyaqem YHWH shoftim vayoshium miyad shoseihem: 'Then YHWH raised up judges, who saved them out of the hand of those who plundered them.' The shofet (judge) in Judges is not primarily a legal arbiter but a military deliverer who rescues Israel in crisis — the role combines elements of prophet (receives the divine call), priest (mediates between YHWH and Israel), and king (leads the people militarily). The Spirit of YHWH coming upon the judges (Othniel, Gideon, Jephthah, Samson) is the OT's primary example of Spirit-empowered charismatic leadership, pointing forward to the permanent anointing of the Spirit on the Davidic king (1 Samuel 16:13) and ultimately on Christ at his baptism (Matthew 3:16).
1: wayyaʿal malʾak YHWH mĕhaggīlgāl ʾel-habbōkîm — 'the angel of YHWH went up from Gilgal to Bochim.' The malʾak YHWH speaks in YHWH's first person in the following verses ('I brought you up,' Judges 2:1; 'I will not drive them out,' Judges 2:3) — the same identification pattern seen in Genesis 16:7-13; Exodus 3:2-6; Joshua 5:13-15. Gilgal was the conquest's covenant-renewal site; the move to Bochim (weepers) signals a new covenantal moment of rebuke.
2: wĕlōʾ tikreṯû bĕrît lĕyôšĕbê hāʾāreṣ... wĕlōʾ šĕmaʿtem bĕqôlî — 'you shall not make covenant... but you have not obeyed my voice.' The covenant-making prohibition (lōʾ tikreṯû bĕrît) was central Sinai instruction (Exodus 34:12-16; Deuteronomy 7:2). The failure formula lōʾ šĕmaʿtem bĕqôlî (you have not obeyed/heard my voice) is Deuteronomy's covenant-obedience formula (Deuteronomy 13:4; Judges 28:1-2). The diagnosis: total covenantal failure in its most critical requirement.
3: ʾāmartî lōʾ-ʾăgārēš ʾōtām mippĕnêkem wĕhāyû lākem lĕṣiddîm — 'I will not drive them out... they shall be thorns in your sides.' Ṣiddîm means literally 'sides, flanks' — persistent lateral pressure rather than frontal assault. The divine reversal is stark: YHWH who promised to gāraš (expel) the nations now withdraws that commitment. Covenant disobedience inverts covenant promises; the expulsion-mandate becomes the abandonment-mandate.
4: wayyiśśĕʾû hāʿām ʾet-qôlām wayyibkû — 'the people lifted up their voices and wept.' The verb bākāh (weep, mourn) signals genuine grief at the covenant indictment. The place-name Bochim (bōkîm) = 'weepers' — named for the response, not the geography. Weeping without structural repentance is the Judges pattern; the tears are real but insufficient to break the cycle.
5: wayyizbaḥû šām laYHWH — 'they sacrificed there to YHWH.' Sacrifice at Bochim marks a covenant-response gesture after the angelic rebuke. The act is genuine but the narrative notes no reform, no removal of foreign gods, no covenant renewal structure — anticipating that the cycle will resume immediately.
6: The narrative flashes back to Joshua's dismissal (repeating Joshua 24:28 verbatim: wayyĕšallaḥ yĕhôšûaʿ ʾet-hāʿām). The flashback brackets the Joshua transition: the generation that saw the great works (kol-hammaʿăśeh haggādôl) is about to pass from the scene. The DtrH inserts the flashback here to calibrate the reader's orientation before the cycle's structural description begins.
7: wayyaʿăbdû hāʿām ʾet-YHWH kol yĕmê yĕhôšûaʿ — 'the people served YHWH all the days of Joshua.' The verb ʿābaḏ (serve, worship) carries both religious and vassal-service connotations. Faithfulness is anchored to living witnesses who saw YHWH's great works (hammāʿăśeh haggādôl). The structural weakness is exposed: covenant loyalty is person-dependent, not character-deep.
8: wayyāmot yĕhôšûaʿ bin-nûn ʿebed YHWH — Joshua died as ʿebed YHWH (servant of the LORD), the title formally conferred in his death notice (Joshua 24:29). The ʿebed YHWH title marks the completion of a servant's mission; Joshua departs as Moses departed — having delivered what he was commissioned to deliver. What follows is explicitly post-servant Israel.
9: Joshua buried at timnāt-ḥeres — a slight variant from Joshua 24:30's timnāt-seraḥ (the consonants ḥ-r-s / s-r-ḥ reversed). Ḥeres can mean 'sun' — Timnath-heres as 'portion of the sun' may be a polemical scribal substitution, as the book is darkly ironic about solar imagery throughout.
10: wĕgam kol-haddôr hahûʾ neʾĕspû ʾel-ʾăbôtāyw wayyāqom dôr ʾaḥēr ʾašer lōʾ-yādĕʿû ʾet-YHWH — 'there arose another generation who did not know the LORD.' The verb yādaʿ in covenant context means relational experiential knowledge (the same yādaʿ as in marital intimacy and prophetic commissioning), not mere intellectual familiarity. Not-knowing YHWH is the theological formulation of covenant abandonment (Hosea 4:1,6; Jeremiah 9:3).
11: wayyaʿăśû bĕnê-yiśrāʾēl ʾet-hāraʿ bĕʿênê YHWH wayyaʿabdû ʾet-habĕʿālîm — 'the evil in YHWH's eyes.' The phrase hāraʿ bĕʿênê YHWH establishes divine perspective as the evaluative norm — all moral assessment in the DtrH is measured against this standard. Habĕʿālîm (the Baals, plural of baʿal, lord/master) are the Canaanite storm-fertility deities whose agricultural domain competed with YHWH's claim over rain and crops (cf. 1 Kgs 18).
12: wayyaʿazĕbû ʾet-YHWH ʾĕlōhê ʾăbôtām — 'they abandoned the LORD, the God of their fathers.' The verb ʿāzab (forsake, abandon) is the covenant-rupture term — the same word for leaving father and mother (Genesis 2:24), applied to the ultimate relational breach. wayyišḥăwû lāhem (bowed down to them) — šāḥāh is the covenant-worship word; prostration that belongs to YHWH alone (Exodus 20:5) is directed to the Baals.
13: wayyaʿazĕbû ʾet-YHWH wayyaʿabdû labbāʿal wĕlāʿaštārôt — Baal and the Ashtoreths. Baʿal (storm/rain deity) and ʿĂštārôt (plural of Astarte/Ishtar, fertility and war goddess) are the paired male-female divine couple of the Canaanite pantheon. Their coupling here establishes the syncretic alternative that will recur as the book's recurring temptation.
14: wayyiḥar-ʾap YHWH bĕyiśrāʾēl wayyittĕnēm bĕyad-šōsîm — 'the anger of YHWH burned and he gave them into the hand of plunderers.' Ḥārāh ʾap (the nostrils burned) is the idiom for intense anger. Nātan bĕyad (gave into the hand) is the conquest-gift formula reversed: what YHWH granted to Israel now goes to its enemies. The covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28:25 are being activated by the operative mechanism of covenant violation.
15: kaʾašer dibber YHWH wĕkaʾašer nišbaʿ YHWH lāhem — 'as YHWH had warned and as YHWH had sworn to them.' The reversal is explicitly grounded in prior divine speech. The sequence — YHWH speaks warning → Israel disobeys → warning comes to pass — confirms that covenant curses have the same word-event reliability as covenant blessings. Nothing falls outside the announced structure of the covenant.
17: wĕgam ʾel-šōpĕṭêhem lōʾ šāmāʿû kî zānû ʾaḥărê ʾĕlōhîm ʾăḥērîm — 'they did not listen... for they committed spiritual adultery (zānāh) after other gods.' The verb zānāh (prostitute oneself, commit harlotry) is the covenant-infidelity metaphor: Israel's relationship with YHWH is a marriage covenant (Hosea 2:2-13; Jeremiah 3:1-5; Ezek 16), and Baal-worship is adultery against the divine husband. The metaphor is not incidental but structurally embedded in covenant theology.
18: ûkî-hēqîm YHWH lāhem šōpĕṭîm wĕhāyāh YHWH ʿim-haššōpēṭ — 'YHWH was with the judge.' The divine-presence formula YHWH ʿimmô is the decisive qualifier in every judge narrative. wĕniḥam YHWH minnāqāt hōšĕqêhem — YHWH relented (nāḥam) because of their groaning. Nāḥam when used of YHWH describes his covenantal responsiveness — not arbitrary changeableness but the covenant structure of cry → compassion → delivery.
19: wĕhāyāh bĕmôt haššōpēṭ yāšûbû wĕhišḥîtû mēʾăbôtām — 'when the judge died, they turned back and were more corrupt than their fathers.' Hišḥîtû (hiph of šāḥat, act corruptly) is the verb used of the Flood generation (Genesis 6:12). The comparative mēʾăbôtām (more than their fathers) marks progressive deterioration across generations — the Judges spiral descends toward the same level of pre-Flood wickedness.
20: yaʿan ʾašer ʿābĕrû hāggôy hazzeh ʾet-bĕrîtî — 'because this nation has transgressed my covenant.' The idiom ʿābar bĕrît (cross/transgress the covenant) uses the spatial verb ʿābar (cross over, pass through) for covenant violation — the covenant is a boundary not to be crossed. ʾašer ṣiwwîtî ʾet-ʾăbôtām establishes the multigenerational binding nature of the Sinai covenant: it obligates every subsequent generation.
21: lōʾ ʾôsîp lĕhôrîš ʾîš mippĕnêhem — 'I will no longer drive out anyone before them.' The withdrawal of the expulsion mandate (hôrîš) is the covenantal consequence of failure to fulfill that mandate. The nations' continued presence, originally a grace-period for gradual conquest, becomes the permanent instrument of ongoing discipline and testing.
22: lĕmaʿan nassôt bām ʾet-yiśrāʾēl hăyišmĕrû ʾet-derek YHWH — 'to test Israel by them, whether they would keep the way of YHWH.' The verb nāsāh (test, prove) is the same as at Moriah (Genesis 22:1) and at the wilderness waters (Exodus 20:20). Testing is pedagogical rather than punitive: it reveals what is already true about the heart. The remaining nations function as an ongoing examination of covenant loyalty under pressure.
23: lōʾ mĕhēr lĕhôrîšām — 'not driving them out quickly.' The adverb mĕhēr (quickly, hastily) is significant: the delay was intentional. YHWH could have expelled them immediately but chose graduated testing. The providential persistence of the nations is the framework for all of Judges' military situations — none fall outside YHWH's intentional governance of the period.
